elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce and develop equivalent spectral graph theory for several fundamental graph cut problems including maxcut, mincut, Cheeger cut, anti-Cheeger cut, dual Cheeger problem and their useful variants.
elements.sent-2411-002: The finer structure of the eigenvectors, the Courant nodal domain theorem and the graphic feature of eigenvalues are studied systematically in the setting of these new nonlinear eigenproblems.
elements.sent-2411-003: A specified strategy for achieving an equivalent eigenproblem is proposed for a general graph cut problem via the Dinkelbach scheme.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also establish several multi-way inequalities relating the variational eigenvalues and min-max $k$-cut problems, which closely resembles the higher-order Cheeger inequality on graph 1-Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-005: Keywords: Cheeger cut, maxcut, dual Cheeger constant, anti-Cheeger cut, nodal domain, $1$-Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-006: Many problems on graph cuts, such as Cheeger cut, maxcut, and balanced minimum cut, are quite important and popular in both pure and applied mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-007: Most of those graph cut problems have rich spectral properties, based on adjacency matrix or Laplacian matrix on graphs, and turns to be the foundation of spectral graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, the linear spectral properties do not capture the graph cuts accurately.
elements.sent-2411-009: For example, sometimes the two nodal domains of the second eigenvector of Laplacian matrix provide a cut that is very different from the Cheeger cut.
elements.sent-2411-010: An instructive example where the nodal domain approximation of the Cheeger cut fails is provided by Guattery and Miller \cite{Guattery/Miller .}
elements.sent-2411-011: A recent line of research indicates that nonlinear spectral graph theory can solve this problem.
elements.sent-2411-012: As a natural discretization of the eigenvalue problem regarding the 1-Laplace operator, the graph 1-Laplacian has been introduced first by Hein and B\"uhler \cite{HeinBuhler2010 for the purpose of application to spectral clustering and then by Chang \cite{refC-JCT for the aim of studying the relatively isoperimetric problem from the variational point of view.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The Cheeger cut and dual Cheeger problem based on graph 1-Laplacian had been studied systematically in both theory and application \cite{refCSZ-JCM,refCSZ-Adv,refC-JCT .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The spectral theory of 1-Laplacian type operator provides tools to analyze Cheeger type constants on graphs, offering insights into the combinatorial optimization through analytic methods.
elements.sent-2411-015: It would be interesting and useful to implement and accomplish similar ideas in other important combinatorial optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-016: Once a combinatorial optimization problem is given, several ``soficity'' questions arise: Is there an equivalent eigenproblem for such combinatorial optimization problem? What is the spectral property of such equivalent eigenproblem? What are the advantages over linear spectral theory? In fact, all the above questions are still open on most of graph optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-017: Even some of fundamental graph cut problems, including maxcut, dual Cheeger cut, anti Cheeger cut, and their modified versions, are not well understood from the spectral perspective.
elements.sent-2411-018: To remedy this, we shall provide equivalent spectral theory for these graph cut problems, and we would focus on the min-max eigenvalues, which closely relate to the higher order versions of these fundamental graph cut problems.
elements.sent-2411-019: We will compare these spectral theories in detail, and highlight some different but useful results.
elements.sent-2411-020: Among a plenty of graph cut problems with some prefixed properties, a handful of 2-cut problems should be basic, for instance,.

For the Cheeger cut or the anti-Cheeger cut, the remaining uncut edges (in black) are divided equally in $S$ and $S^c$ (balanced and judicious!). For the mincut or the maxcut, the remaining uncut edges are all contained in one part (biased!). } \label{fig:f4-cut}
elements.sent-2411-021: By the description in Fig.~\ref{fig:f4-cut , the Cheeger cut and the anti-Cheeger cut can be regarded as ``balanced'' versions of the mincut and the maxcut, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Since the standard mincut problem can be solved in polynomial time, we won't pay much attention to it, and instead we will systematically study another balanced variant of the maxcut problem (called the dual Cheeger problem) in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-023: Before stating the related spectral graph theory, let us recall the fundamental graph cut problems that will be systematically studied in this paper:.
elements.sent-2411-024: The standard Cheeger constant of an undirected graph $G=(V,E)$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{prob:Cheeger} h(G)=\min\limits_{S\subseteq V,S\not\in\{\emptyset,V\}} \frac{|E( S,S^c)|}{\min\{\vol(S),\vol(S^c)\}}\end{equation} where $V=\{1,\cdots,n\}$ is the vertex set, $\vol(S)$ is the sum of vertex weights of vertices in $S$, and $|E(S,S^c)|$ counts the total edge weights of edges acrossing $S$ and $S^c$.
elements.sent-2411-025: While any solution $S$ of the combinatorial minimization problem \eqref{prob:Cheeger gives a Cheeger cut $(S,S^c)$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The very famous maxcut problem is a combinatorial optimization defined by \begin{equation}\label{prob:maxcut} h_{\max}(G)= \max\limits_{S\subseteq V,S\not\in\{\emptyset,V\}}\frac{2|E(S,S^c)| }{\vol(V)}\end{equation} and a cut $(S,S^c)$ realizing \eqref{prob:maxcut is called a maxcut.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We note here that the {\sl mincut problem is simply defined by using $\min$ instead of $\max$ in \eqref{prob:maxcut .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The dual Cheeger constant \cite{Trevisan,Bauer-Jost is simply defined by \begin{equation}h^+(G)=\max\limits_{V_1\cap V_2=\varnothing,V_1\cup V_2\ne \varnothing} \frac{2|E(V_1,V_2)|}{\vol(V_1\cup V_2)},\label{eq:dual-Cheeger-constant}\end{equation} A useful variant of the dual Cheeger problem \eqref{eq:dual-Cheeger-constant is first presented in \cite{Soto and then in \cite{SYZ , \begin{equation}\widehat{h}^+(G)=\max\limits_{V_1\cap V_2=\varnothing,V_1\cup V_2\ne \varnothing} \frac{2|E(V_1,V_2)|+|E\left(V_1\cup V_2,(V_1\cup V_2)^c\right)|}{\vol(V_1\cup V_2)+|E\left(V_1\cup V_2,(V_1\cup V_2)^c\right)|}.\label{eq:modified-dual-Cheeger-constant}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\widehat{h}^+(G)=\max\limits_{V_1\cap V_2=\varnothing,V_1\cup V_2\ne \varnothing} \frac{2|E(V_1,V_2)|+|E\left(V_1\cup V_2,(V_1\cup V_2)^c\right)|}{\vol(V_1\cup V_2)+|E\left(V_1\cup V_2,(V_1\cup V_2)^c\right)|}.\label{eq:modified-dual-Cheeger-constant}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-033: Theories of spectra on graphs are arguably one of the most important directions in graph theory in the last decade.
elements.sent-2411-034: They link graph theory to spectral theory, and through this connection introduce new tools to graph theory.
elements.sent-2411-035: Let's start with the spectrum of graph Laplacian, which provides good lower and upper bounds for the Cheeger constant.
elements.sent-2411-036: For instance, let $\lambda_k(\Delta_2)$ denote the $k$-th smallest eigenvalue of the normalized graph Laplacian $\Delta_2$, where $k=1,2,\cdots,n$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Then there holds the famous Cheeger inequality \cite{Alon86,Alon-Milman,Dodziuk84,Fiedler \begin{equation}\label{eq:Cheeger-inequality} \frac{h^2(G)}{2}\le \lambda_2(\Delta_2)\le 2h(G).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-038: We remark that the proof of the left-hand-side inequality (Cheeger's part) above is far from obvious, while the proof of the right-hand-side inequality (Buser's part) is surprisingly simple.
elements.sent-2411-039: Whilst the above Cheeger inequality gives a two-sided sharp bound for the Cheeger constant by the easily computable second Laplacian eigenvalue, neither the eigenvalue nor the eigenvector solve the Cheeger cut problem on general graphs.
elements.sent-2411-040: With regard to different extensions of the Cheeger inequality, the dual version \begin{equation}\label{eq:dual-Cheeger-inequality} \frac{\big(1-h^+(G)\big)^2}{2}\le 2-\lambda_n(\Delta_2)\le 2\big(1-h^+(G)\big)\end{equation} established by Trevisan \cite{Trevisan , and independently, by Bauer and Jost \cite{Bauer-Jost , seems quite fundamental.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Regarding the Laplacian spectrum bound for maxcut, Delorme and Poljak \cite{DelormePoljak93 give an inequality \begin{equation}h_{\max}(G)\le \frac n4 \lambda_n(L+U)\end{equation} where $\lambda_n(L+U)$ is the largest eigenvalue of the matrix $L+U$, $L$ is the unnormalized Laplacian of $G$ and $U$ is any given diagonal matrix with zero trace.}
elements.sent-2411-042: It is easy to slightly modify Delorme-Poljak's inequality by using the normalized Laplacian $\Delta_2$: \begin{equation}h_{\max}(G)\le \frac{\vol(V)}{4} \lambda_n(\Delta_2+D^{-1}U)\end{equation} where $D$ is the diagonal matrix of degrees.
elements.sent-2411-044: These results from spectral graph theory have proven to be extremely useful to derive eigenvalue bounds for certain graph cut problems and graph parameters.
elements.sent-2411-045: However, the classical spectral graph theory based on graph matrices (e.g., adjacency matrix and Laplace matrix) never provide the `correct'\footnote{We shall give a footnote here to explain what `correct' means in our context.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Linear spectral theory is not `correct' in that the eigenvalues do not exactly represent the cut value, the eigenvectors need rounding to get back to the original problem, and the method of rounding the eigenvectors is not easy, and moreover, the resulting cut does not necessarily still have the original inequality relation.
elements.sent-2411-047: In contrast, our equivalent nonlinear spectral theory is `correct' in that the eigenvectors are completely equivalent to the solution of the graph cut problem, and thus do not require any rounding.
elements.sent-2411-048: Fortunately, the $\ell^1$-type nonlinear spectral graph theory partially solves the problem, in which a fundamental fact is: All the above graph cut problems can be equivalently transformed to certain eigenproblems of 1-Laplacian type.
elements.sent-2411-049: We shall explain the above slogan in detail.
elements.sent-2411-050: It has been proved that the Cheeger constant $h(G)$ equals the second eigenvalue of the set-valued eigenproblem \begin{equation}\label{eq:1-lap} 0\in\Delta_1\vec x-\lambda D\mathrm{Sgn}(x)\end{equation} which is called `1-Laplacian' eigenequation in \cite{HeinBuhler2010,refC-JCT .}
elements.sent-2411-051: And the Cheeger cut is equivalent to the second eigenvector of \eqref{eq:1-lap , which will be discussed in Sections \ref{sec:Cheeger .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Thus, if we work on 1-Laplacian instead of the normalized Laplacian, the Cheeger inequality \eqref{eq:Cheeger-inequality becomes an equality, and the disadvantage that the Laplacian eigenvectors do not provide Cheeger cut automatically disappears.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Similar situation occurs for the dual Cheeger problem.
elements.sent-2411-054: We prove that the solution of the dual Cheeger problem is equivalent to the first eigenpair of the so-called `signless 1-Laplacian' eigenequation \cite{SYZ : \begin{equation}\label{eq:signless-1} 0\in\Delta_1^+\vec x-\lambda D\mathrm{Sgn}(x) .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We continue the work in \cite{SYZ to provide estimates for higher order eigenvalues.}
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, we establish a higher order Cheeger inequality, and provide a relation between the Laplacian spectrum and the signless 1-Laplacian eigenvalue \eqref{eq:signless-1 (see Theorems \ref{thm:k-way-dual-Cheeger and \ref{thm:2Lap-s1Lap ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: A remarkable result we would like to highlight is that the multiplicity of the largest eigenvalue of $\Delta_1^+$ is at least the independence number $\alpha$, and at most the edge covering number (see Theorem \ref{thm:multi-of-1 ), which has no analogous on the traditional linear spectrum.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In particular, if the graph is bipartite, then the eigenvalue multiplicity equals $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Another noteworthy result concerns the case of equalities on higher order Cheeger inequality: If the graph is a tree, then the $k$-way dual Cheeger constant plus the $k$-th min-max eigenvalue of $\Delta_1^+$ equals 1, which has no analogous result in linear spectral theory.
elements.sent-2411-060: This equality is a consequence of the sharp nodal count theory established in \cite{DPT23 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: We also point out that since the property of $\Delta_1^+$ is quite different from $\Delta_1$, making the strong nodal domain theorem no longer valid for $\Delta_1^+$, we employ an alternative natural definition of nodal domains to ensure the validity of Courant's theorem.

elements.sent-2411-063: Besides, the mincut problem, and the min-max $k$-cut are also encoded in the spectral information of \eqref{eq:1-lap-maxcut .}
elements.sent-2411-064: We refer to Theorem \ref{thm:ck-maxkcut for the connection between variational eigenvalues of \eqref{eq:1-lap-maxcut and min-max $k$-cut proposed in Definition \ref{defn:minmax-k-cut .}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: We would like to highlight that by introducing new versions of nodal domain, we actually strengthen the Courant nodal domain theorem on \eqref{eq:1-lap-maxcut , see Section \ref{sec:maxcut .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Another main contribution of this work is a new equivalent spectral theory for the Cheeger constant, which is closely related to, but not the same as, the graph 1-Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-067: We prove that the eigenvalues of the 1-Laplacian eigenproblem \eqref{eq:1-lap are always the eigenvalues of the eigenproblem \begin{equation}\label{eq:new-Cheeger-equi} 0\in e\partial\|\vec x\|_\infty-\Delta_1^+\vec x-\lambda\partial N(\vec x)\end{equation} and the second eigenvalue of \eqref{eq:new-Cheeger-equi equals the Cheeger constant, see Section \ref{sec:new-Cheeger .}}}

elements.sent-2411-073: To facilitate the reader's quick understanding of our results, in this subsection, we briefly review the graph 1-Laplacian, in which some of the details had been collected in \cite{HeinBuhler2010,refC-JCT .}
elements.sent-2411-074: The graph 1-Laplacian $\Delta_1$, defined as the subgradient of the total variation functional can be written in coordinate form: \begin{equation}(\Delta_1\vec x)_i\coloneqq\left\{\left.\sum_{j\in V:\{i,j\}\in E}w_{ij}z_{ij}\right|{l}z_{ij}\in Sgn(x_i-x_j), z_{ij}=-z_{ji}  \right\},\;i\in V\end{equation} where\begin{equation}Sgn(t)\coloneqq  \{1\} if  t>0, [-1,1] if t=0, \{-1\} if t<0. \end{equation} The eigenvalue problem for $\Delta_1$ consists in finding $\lambda\in\R$ and $\vec x\ne \vec 0$ such that \begin{equation}\Delta_1\vec x\bigcap \lambda \vec\muSgn(\vec x)\ne\emptyset\end{equation} where $\mathrm{Sgn (\vec x)=\{(y_1,\cdots,y_n):y_i\in\mathrm{Sgn (x_i),i\in V\}$, $\vec\mu:=\mathrm{diag (\mu_1,\cdots,\mu_n)$ with $\mu_i$ being the weight of the vertex $i$.}}}
